title: The King Who Settled Accounts
subtitle: The servant forgiven ten thousand talents, who would not forgive a hundred pence

stillness: Be still, and let your own conscience be examined.
For He who is Lord and King of all things wills to settle accounts with you.
We are now within that examination.
gospel: The kingdom of heaven is likened to a man that is a king,
who would take an account of his servants.
And when he had begun to take the account,
one was brought to him that owed him ten thousand talents.
And as he had not wherewith to pay it,
his lord commanded that he should be sold,
and his wife, and his children, and all that he had, and payment to be made.
But that servant falling down, besought him, saying:
Have patience with me, and I will pay thee all.
And the lord of that servant, being moved with pity,
let him go and forgave him the debt.
But when that servant was gone out,
he found one of his fellow-servants that owed him a hundred pence;
and laying hold of him, he throttled him, saying: Pay what thou owest.
behold: Behold the king who wills to settle accounts with his servants, that is, with men, whose Lord He is.
For this is Christ, true man and true God, King of kings and Lord of lords.
Behold the one brought before him who owed ten thousand talents:
in that debt is shown the gravity and the multitude of his sins.
He could not pay; for man of himself can fall, but not rise again,
of himself can offend and sin, but not of himself make satisfaction, unless the grace of God draw near.
Behold him falling down, the sinner, who before had stiffened his neck against God,
now adoring, beseeching: Have patience with me, and I will pay thee all.
And the Lord, being merciful, ready to spare the truly penitent,
loosed him free from the captivity of his guilt, and forgave him the whole debt.
He had asked only for delay, and received the remission of all his sins.
But now behold him gone out: freed from his debt, yet the servant of iniquity,
and forgetful of the mercy he had found.
He finds a fellow-servant who owed him a hundred pence — a small thing, a trifle,
for an offence against a man is small beside the offence committed against God.
And laying hold of him, he throttled him.
To throttle is to strangle, to choke by the throat;
and he throttles his neighbour who will not hear the words of his excusing.
He holds his debtor who holds the offence in his memory, who keeps wrath and hatred in his heart.
Pay what thou owest, he says — torment for injury, blows for words.
And mark this: his fellow-servant fell down and spoke the very same words —
Have patience with me, and I will pay thee all —
the very words by which this wicked servant had himself earned remission from his Lord.
Yet the ungrateful man stood in no awe of the words by which he was saved.
consider: We are greater debtors to God, and offend Him more, than any man could offend his neighbour.
So great is the offence as is He who is offended:
and God is infinite, and therefore the offence committed against Him has a certain infinity.
And yet, as often as we offend, so often is He ready to forgive — if only we will truly repent.
If He, the Lord and King of all, forgives His servant,
how much more must we forgive the brother and neighbour who sins against us.
The Lord will set us to our account: for all the good entrusted to us, and the good we have left undone;
for the evil we have done, and the evil we have failed to put away.
If we had to render account for our time alone, it would be much to be feared:
for every hour granted to man shall be required of him, how it was spent —
and in one hour of time a man may be absolved of his debt, and gain the kingdom of heaven.
This reckoning is much to be feared, because of the vice of ingratitude, which is exceedingly perilous.
He holds his debtor who keeps the injury alive in memory, and guards wrath and hatred in his heart.
To throttle a fellow-servant is to burn against a brother through vengeance.
He would not have patience, nor grant delay, nor let go his rancour without revenge.
prayer: We run after Thee, O Lord Jesus,
hearing that Thou dost not spurn the poor, nor abhor the sinner.
Thou didst not abhor the thief confessing Thee,
nor the woman weeping, nor the Canaanite woman pleading,
nor her taken in adultery, nor him sitting at the toll-booth,
nor the publican beseeching, nor the disciple denying,
nor the persecutor of Thy disciples, nor Thy very crucifiers.
So mighty art Thou at last to justify, as Thou art abundant to forgive.
The bride runs, the maidens run;
but she who loves more ardently runs more swiftly, and arrives the sooner.

latinJewel.latin: Tam validus denique es ad justificandum, quam multus ad ignoscendum.
latinJewel.english: So mighty art Thou, at the last, to justify, as Thou art abundant to forgive.
